file_name: Zareen et al. - 2022 - A machine vision approach for classification of skin cancer using hybrid texture features.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a machine vision approach for the classification of five types of skin cancer (actinic-keratosis, benign, solar-lentigo, malignant, and nevus). It employed hybrid texture features (first-order histogram and GLCM), dimensionality reduction via PCA and CFS, and machine learning classifiers including Naive Bayes, Bayes Net, LMT Tree, and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), achieving 97.1333% accuracy with MLP on the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the collection of a 1000-image ISIC dataset, extraction of hybrid texture features (first-order histogram and GLCM), dimensionality reduction using PCA and CFS, and classification using NB, BN, LMT Tree, and MLP classifiers, achieving a reported accuracy of 97.1333%.

title: A Machine Vision Approach for Classification of Skin Cancer Using Hybrid Texture Features
brief_summary: This study developed a machine vision approach to classify five types of skin cancer (actinic-keratosis, benign, solar-lentigo, malignant, and nevus) using hybrid texture features. The researchers utilized a benchmark dataset of 1,000 images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). Feature extraction involved combining first-order histogram and gray level co-occurrence matrix (GLCM) features, followed by dimensionality reduction using PCA and correlation-based feature selection (CFS) to optimize 12 features. The optimized features were classified using Naive Bayes, Bayes Net, LMT Tree, and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) algorithms with 10-fold cross-validation. The MLP classifier achieved the highest overall accuracy of 97.1333%, demonstrating superior performance compared to other tested classifiers and existing methods.

all_task_types.target: Five types of skin cancer (actinic-keratosis, benign, solar-lentigo, malignant, and nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin cancer using hybrid texture features. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for five specific skin cancer types. The primary classifier (MLP) achieved an overall accuracy of 97.1333% on the ISIC dataset using 10-fold cross-validation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A benchmark image dataset of skin cancers collected from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) available online. The dataset consists of 1000 images (200 images per class) across five types of skin cancer. Images were compressed to 512x512 pixel resolution and converted to 8-bit grayscale BMP format.

use.training: Used for training classifiers (NB, BN, LMT Tree, MLP) via 10-fold cross-validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classification algorithms (NB, BN, LMT Tree, MLP) on the optimized feature datasets.
To reduce the dimensionality of the feature dataset and select the most discriminative features.
To further reduce feature dimensionality and select an optimized subset of 12 features.
Used as a statistical method for feature selection/optimization to evaluate class separability.

statistical_methods_used.method: Average Correlation Coefficient plus Probability of Error (ACC + POE)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used as a statistical method for feature selection/optimization.
To measure the agreement between the predicted and actual classifications, correcting for chance.
To evaluate the average magnitude of errors in the predictions.
To evaluate the diagnostic ability of the classifiers across different threshold settings.
To calculate per-class accuracy, miss classification rate, true positive, false positive, sensitivity, and specificity.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, confusion matrix metrics (sensitivity, specificity, etc.), and cross-validation, without statistical significance testing between classifiers.

ISIC Dataset.experiment: 10-fold cross-validation on optimized feature dataset (12 features)
ISIC Dataset.notes: The MLP classifier achieved the highest overall accuracy of 97.1333%. The total number of instances for all classes was 3000 (600 per class). The study compared these results against existing approaches in Table 6, noting higher accuracy than Kumar et al. (95%), Esteva et al. (72.1 ± 0.9), Shena et al. (78.4), Yasir et al. (90), Haenssle et al. (86.6), and Kawahara et al. (40.8% to 91%).

training_details.feature_extraction: Hybrid texture features (First-order histogram and GLCM)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: GoogleNet Inception V-3 (referenced as Esteva et al.)
Google's Inception v4 CNN (referenced as Haenssle et al.)
Image-net model (referenced as Kawahara et al.)
preprocessing_techniques: Conversion from JPEG to 8-bit grayscale BMP format
Laplacian filter application for noise reduction and image quality enhancement
Normalization of pixel values (mapping to positive integer 1 byte)
Region of Interest (ROI) extraction using retrieve-greedy-ROI-based algorithm
Creation of three non-overlapping circular ROIs per image
conclusions: The study concludes that a machine vision approach using hybrid texture features (first-order histogram and GLCM) is effective for classifying five types of skin cancer (actinic-keratosis, benign, solar-lentigo, malignant, and nevus).

The Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) classifier achieved the highest overall accuracy of 97.1333% among the tested classifiers (NB, BN, LMT Tree, and MLP) on the ISIC dataset using 10-fold cross-validation.

The MLP classifier demonstrated superior performance compared to Naive Bayes (85.9667%), Bayes Net (92.1333%), and LMT Tree (95.9667%) in terms of accuracy and lower miss-classification rate (2.8667%).

The proposed method outperformed several existing approaches cited in the literature, including Kumar et al. (95%), Esteva et al. (72.1 ± 0.9), Shena et al. (78.4), Yasir et al. (90), Haenssle et al. (86.6), and Kawahara et al. (40.8% to 91%).
The MLP classifier achieved per-class accuracies of 95.5% for actinic-keratosis, 94.8333% for benign, 97% for solar-lentigo, 98.3333% for malignant, and 100% for nevus.
The study acknowledges that the MLP classifier performed better with complex, noisy, and huge datasets compared to other machine vision classifiers.
The use of PCA and Correlation-based Feature Selection (CFS) for dimensionality reduction and feature optimization was found to be beneficial for achieving high classification accuracy.
The study highlights the importance of automated diagnostic methods to reduce the time and cost associated with manual skin cancer diagnosis and to assist dermatologists.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that the work was initially available as a preprint on SSRN and had not been peer-reviewed at the time of publication.
The dataset consists of only 1,000 images (200 per class) from the ISIC archive, which is a relatively small sample size for deep learning or robust machine learning validation.

The 'Nevus' class achieved 100% accuracy with zero false positives in the confusion matrix, which may indicate class separability issues or potential data leakage/bias within the specific 10-fold cross-validation splits.

The paper compares its results against existing literature (e.g., Esteva et al., Haenssle et al.) using overall accuracy metrics, but does not provide a direct head-to-head comparison on the same held-out test set, limiting the validity of these comparative claims.

The feature extraction process involved converting images to grayscale and extracting features from three non-overlapping ROIs, which may discard color information potentially relevant for dermatological diagnosis.
The MLP classifier had the longest training time (6.6 seconds) compared to other classifiers (NB: 0.05s, BN: 0.85s, LMT Tree: 0.47s), which is a consideration for deployment efficiency.

No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely using 10-fold cross-validation on the single ISIC dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
